       CMDR-DOS could be easily ported to other Commodore-like 65c02+ systems like the  MEGA65 and the  C256 Foenix, providing a DOS interface to FAT32 on those platforms.
  Functionality-wise, the CMDR-DOS codebase is also very similar to what sd2iec does – minus the Commodore Serial part. It could be ported a device like the  1581replica, with an SD card attached instead of a disk drive, and one would have a 65c02-based sdi2ec-like device.
title: Cmdr-DOS：FAT32上的Commodore DOS

note:
1964年7月，在曼哈顿上东区，休班的白人警官托马斯·吉利根枪杀了黑人青少年詹姆斯·鲍威尔。哈莱姆和贝德福德-斯图文森爆发了起义，持续了六个晚上，然后在全国范围内引发了抗议活动。黑人女权主义作家和活动家琼·乔丹在她的书“内战”的前言中写道，在抗议活动之后，“我意识到我现在充满了对一切和每个白人的仇恨。几乎同时，我突然意识到，如果这种情况持续下去，就意味着我失去了重点…。我决心不再怀恨在心，而是为了我所爱的人，用我所爱的语言。然而，在我的人民之外，我不知道我爱的内容是什么：我是用来做什么的？“。
乔丹立即给出了答案，投入了她所说的“哈莱姆区的合作建筑重新设计”，在这个项目中，她与测地线穹顶的拥护者、建筑师R·巴克明斯特·富勒(R.Buckminster Fuller)联手。乔丹和富勒称他们的合作是“哈莱姆区的摩天大楼”：一项公共住房计划，符合该社区25万居民的福祉，其中大多数是黑人。对于乔丹来说，这个项目可能看起来是一个左转，她通过散文和诗歌而声名鹊起。但她一直认为自己的工作属于环境设计的范畴--“也就是说，”她解释说，“总的来说，是为积极改变世界做出贡献的努力。”
特别是建筑，长期以来一直是这位年轻艺术家的赖以生存的源泉。四年前，乔丹是一位精疲力竭的24岁母亲，有一个蹒跚学步的孩子，住在皇后区的一个住房项目里。一位试图说服她去度一个急需的假期的朋友带着关于希腊的图画书顺道来访。乔丹后来写道，这是一次“令人惊叹的视觉洪流”。“我就是这样开始思考建筑的。”她开始了每周一次的例行公事。每周有一次，她把两岁的儿子交给丈夫照看，然后乘坐进入曼哈顿的公交车来到唐纳尔图书馆(Donnell Library)，在那里她在楼下的阅览室里坐了几个小时，埋头阅读关于建筑和设计的书籍：勒·柯布西耶(Le Corbusier)、野口勇(Isamu Noguchi)、包豪斯(Bauhaus)和富勒(Fuller)，后者是一位经济适用、可持续住房的远见卓识者。乔丹写道，“富勒的想法压在了我自己身上，这是一种尚未押注于美国景观的预感，在那里，随着白人针对黑人生活的暴力升级，人们在恐惧中获得的皮肤每天都会出现致命的两极分化。”
起义恰逢约旦生活中的动荡时期。骚乱发生一周后，乔丹的丈夫写信说，他不会再回他们的家了；越来越穷困潦倒的乔丹，把她的儿子送到了他的祖父母那里。她写信给富勒，他几乎立即回复，他们花了几个月的时间起草了“哈莱姆区的摩天大楼”，这是一个社区的计划，这个社区的居民长期受到持续的治安、狭窄的宿舍和破旧的学校的影响。他们的计划将在不迁移任何现有居民的情况下改变哈莱姆区，这些居民经常成为“城市重建”(或乔丹和其他人所说的“黑人迁移”)的附带损害。城市重建涉及将某些地区指定为“枯萎病”-这一术语不成比例地适用于低收入的黑人和棕色社区-以证明拆除现有结构和授权新建建筑是合理的。罗伯特·摩西(Robert Mosse)就是这种做法的例证，例如，他现在臭名昭著的跨布朗克斯高速公路(Cross Bronx Expressway)依赖于否认东特雷蒙特丰富的文化网络和微观经济，这些网络和微观经济后来被高速公路的建设摧毁了。
与贬低黑人和棕色人口的城市重建项目相比，富勒和乔丹的设计试图改变环境，为哈莱姆区的居民服务。该计划雄心勃勃，但需要采取严厉的措施。“局部翻修是不够的，”乔丹写道。“半个世纪的绝望需要驱魔。”安装在后院的柱子将充当高跷，这样就可以在现有建筑上方建造15座防火的圆锥形塔楼。这些塔楼将包括新的居住空间-1200平方英尺光线充足的公寓，每个公寓都配有阳台和停车位-以及工作室、音乐厅、剧院、运动场和娱乐空间。停车坡道和吊桥将穿过塔楼，绿地和集体休闲区将扩大。在建筑完成后，住在下面楼宇的居民便会简单地搬到经改善的单位。居民入住新单位后，旧单位将“改建为公共、休憩、停车等空地”。
该提案-强调汽车、高速公路和高层公共住房-与摩西的愿景有一定的共同点。
“时尚先生”寄来的支票是1964年12月24日寄来的。“我恳求银行立即兑现这张支票，”乔丹回忆道。然后她前往机场接她的儿子，儿子正好在圣诞节前赶回家。
“哈莱姆区的天空升起”从未登上过版面。尽管乔丹坚称两人完全期待该计划的实施，但对富勒来说，它的命运并不是异常的，他壮观的想法经常超过他帮助他们实现的承诺。然而，与富勒的许多其他头脑风暴不同的是，与“天际升起”的接触一直是零星的。一些消息来源已经报道了该项目，但没有透露约旦的功劳。富勒的当地报纸“南伊利诺伊州报”(Southern Illinoisan)在1965年的一篇文章中描述了这一提议，并将这一建议完全归功于富勒。惠特尼2008年的展览“巴克敏斯特·富勒：从宇宙开始”(Buckminster Fuller：Start With the Universal)包括富勒的同事佐藤昭二(Shoji Sadao)的蓝图，该蓝图与“绅士”(Esquire)的文章一起出现，但没有提到乔丹。乔丹在“内战”中写到了这个项目的起源，以及她对“时尚先生”的编辑改动的失望。
哈佛大学施莱辛格图书馆6月份的乔丹论文包含了该项目最有力的文件。基于他们的合作，富勒为乔丹写了罗马大奖赛的推荐信，这封推荐信授予她环境设计方面的奖学金。在罗马，乔丹开始研究后来成为“现在就好”(OK Now)的小说，这是一部仍未出版的小说，想象了密西西比州鲁里维尔(Ruleville)的一个激进的农业公社。尽管乔丹-1973年考入耶鲁艺术与建筑学院(Yale School Of Art And Architecture)-最终摆脱了传统的设计研究，但她的作品证明了她对将设计作为一种变质实践的持续忠诚。从“OK Now”到她的报纸报道，再到她2000年的回忆录“士兵：诗人的童年”(Soldier：A Poet‘s Childhood)，乔丹的写作特点是对建筑环境和社会意义的纠缠给予深度关注，并致力于创造一种空间秩序，正如她在给富勒的一封信中所写的那样，黑人可以参与到“他们自己的现实的诞生”中。
尽管“哈莱姆的天空升起”并没有建成，但它并没有失败；它是约旦更大的、终生文学项目的一部分，在这个反对警察暴行和其他形式的反黑人种族主义的起义中，这一点清晰得令人共鸣。她所谓的“形式诗学”反对将黑人与枯萎病联系在一起的种族主义隐喻。(例如，摩西关于跨布朗克斯高速公路的计划，动员了一系列种族主义想法，以获得对他的项目的支持。)。产生了对“哈莱姆区的摩天大楼”的迫切需求的世界，正是这个世界取消了其实现的抵押品赎回权。但正如地理学教授、监狱废奴主义者鲁思·威尔逊·吉尔摩(Ruth Wilson Gilmore)所写的那样，“世界将会变成什么样子，已经存在于碎片、实验和可能性之中。”乔丹实验的碎片可能会缝合到我们自己破碎开放的时刻。
note_en:
In July of 1964, on the Upper East Side of Manhattan, Thomas Gilligan, a white off‐duty police officer, shot and killed James Powell, a Black teen-ager. Uprisings erupted in Harlem and Bedford-Stuyvesant, which lasted six nights and then ignited protests across the nation. In the foreword to her book “ Civil Wars,” the Black feminist writer and activist June Jordan wrote that, in the aftermath of the protests, “I realized I now was filled with hatred for everything and everyone white. Almost simultaneously it came to me that this condition, if it lasted, would mean I had lost the point. . . . I resolved not to run on hatred but, instead, to use what I loved, words, for the sake of the people I loved. However, beyond my people, I did not know the content of my love: what was I  for?”
  Jordan immediately provided an answer by throwing herself into what she called “a collaborative architectural redesign of Harlem,” in which she joined forces with the  architect R. Buckminster Fuller, champion of the geodesic dome. Jordan and Fuller called their collaboration “Skyrise for Harlem”: a plan for public housing that was attuned to the well-being of two hundred and fifty thousand of the neighborhood’s residents, most of them Black. The project may have seemed a left turn for Jordan, who came to prominence through her essays and poetry. But she had always conceived of her work as falling under the umbrella of environmental design—“that is,” she explained, “in general, an effort to contribute to the positive changing of the world.”
 Architecture, in particular, had long been a source of sustenance for the young artist. Four years earlier, Jordan was an exhausted twenty-four-year-old mother of a toddler living in a housing project in Queens. A friend who was trying to convince her to take a much-needed vacation dropped by with picture books about Greece in tow. It was a “fantastic visual inundation,” Jordan later wrote. “It was in this way that I began to think about architecture.” She began a weekly routine. Once a week, she left her two-year-old son in the care of her husband and took the bus into Manhattan to the Donnell Library, where she sat for hours in the downstairs reading room, poring over books about architecture and design: Le Corbusier, Isamu Noguchi, the Bauhaus, and Fuller, a visionary of affordable, sustainable housing. Jordan wrote, “Fuller’s thinking weighed upon my own as a hunch yet to be gambled on the American landscape where daily, deathly polarization of peoples according to skin gained in horror as white violence escalated against Black life.”
  The uprisings coincided with a turbulent period in Jordan’s life. A week after the riots, Jordan’s husband wrote to say that he wouldn’t be returning to their home; Jordan, increasingly destitute, sent her son to his grandparents. She wrote to Fuller, he responded almost immediately, and they spent several months drafting “Skyrise for Harlem,” a plan for a neighborhood where residents had long been subjected to constant policing, cramped quarters, and dilapidated schools. Their plan would transform Harlem without displacing any of its existing residents, who often became the collateral damage of “urban renewal” (or what Jordan and others called “Negro removal”). Urban renewal involves the designation of certain areas as “blight”—a term disproportionately applied to low-income Black and brown communities—in order to justify demolition of existing structures and authorize new building. The practice was exemplified by Robert Moses, whose now-infamous Cross Bronx Expressway, for example, relied on denying the rich cultural networks and microeconomies of East Tremont, which were then destroyed by the highway’s construction.
 In contrast with urban-renewal projects that devalued Black and brown populations, Fuller and Jordan’s design sought to transform the environment in service of Harlem’s residents. The plan was ambitious, but drastic measures were required. “Partial renovation is not enough,” Jordan wrote. “A half century of despair requires exorcism.” Columns installed in backyards would act as stilts so that construction of fifteen fireproof, conical towers could take place above existing buildings. These towers would contain new dwelling space—light-filled apartments of twelve-hundred square feet, each equipped with a balcony and parking spot—as well as studios, concert halls, theatres, athletic fields, and recreational space. Parking ramps and suspension bridges would cut through the towers, and green space and collective leisure areas would be expanded. After construction was completed, the residents who lived in the buildings below would simply move up to the improved units. After residents had settled into their new units, the old units would be “converted into communal, open space for recreation, parking and so forth.”
  The proposal—with its emphasis on cars and highways and high-rise public housing—shared certain tendencies with Moses’s visions. But what critically distinguished “Skyrise for Harlem” from urban-renewal projects, in addition to its commitment to resisting displacement, was the emphasis on residents’ imaginations. This was a plan attentive to the creative possibilities of interior life and social space. “Every room has a view,” Jordan wrote, noting that views would offer a counterpoint to the city’s density; residents might look onto the river and dream a while.
  Under her married name, Meyer, Jordan wrote about “ Skyrise for Harlem” in the April, 1965, issue of  Esquire. Jordan chaffed against  Esquire’s stipulations. “The limitation of 2500 words seems to me arbitrary and acceptable only if it becomes possible to adequately condense to a poetry of form the verbal aspect of the piece,” she wrote to Fuller, emphasizing the project’s allegiance to radical imagination. In the article, Jordan omitted her own integral role in the project—perhaps to seize on the celebrity of her collaborator, who had appeared on the cover of  Time the previous year. “Fuller’s design,” “Fuller’s circular decked towers,” “Fuller’s solution,” Jordan wrote. Still, their shared enthusiasm for the transformative potential of design comes through: “There is no evading architecture, no meaningful denial of our position. You can build to defend the endurance of man, to protect his existence, to illuminate it. But you cannot build for these purposes merely in spasmodic response to past and present crises, for then crisis, like the poor, will be with us always.”
 Jordan submitted the article under the headline “Skyrise for Harlem,” but the editors replaced it with one of their own, “Instant Slum Clearance,” which encapsulated precisely the dominant urban-planning idea that Jordan and Fuller’s design rejected: that Black residents were a form of contamination who had to be removed for a neighborhood to flourish. The subtitle—“R. Buckminster Fuller designs a total solution to an American dilemma: here, for instance, is how it would work in Harlem”—clinched the project’s attribution to Fuller and reversed Jordan’s guiding question, “What was I  for?” It cast the plan from one motivated by the love of a particular place into one preoccupied with a generalized violence.
 The check from  Esquire arrived on December 24, 1964. “I pleaded with the bank to cash the check, immediately,” Jordan recalled. Then she headed to the airport to pick up her son, who made it home just in time for Christmas.
 “Skyrise for Harlem” never made it off the page. Although Jordan insisted that the pair fully expected the plan to be carried out, its fate was hardly an anomaly for Fuller, whose spectacular ideas regularly outpaced his commitment to seeing them through. Unlike many of Fuller’s other brainstorms, however, engagements with “Skyrise” have been scattershot. A few sources have covered the project, without giving credit to Jordan. A 1965 article in the Southern Illinoisan, Fuller’s local newspaper, described the proposal, giving sole credit to Fuller. The Whitney’s exhibition “Buckminster Fuller: Starting with the Universe,” from 2008, included the blueprint by Fuller’s associate Shoji Sadao that appeared alongside the Esquire article, with no mention of Jordan. Jordan wrote about the project’s genesis and her frustration with Esquire’s editorial changes in “Civil Wars.”
  The June Jordan Papers, at Harvard’s Schlesinger Library, contain the project’s most robust documentation. Based on their collaboration, Fuller wrote Jordan’s letter of recommendation for a Prix de Rome, which awarded her a fellowship in environmental design. In Rome, Jordan began research for what would become “Okay Now,” a still-unpublished novel that imagines a radical farming commune in Ruleville, Mississippi. Though Jordan—who, in 1973, was admitted to the Yale School of Art and Architecture—eventually moved away from the conventional study of design, her work bears out a sustained allegiance to design as a metamorphic practice. From “Okay Now” to her newspaper reporting to her memoir, “ Soldier: A Poet’s Childhood,” from 2000, Jordan’s writing is characterized by deep attention to the entanglements of the built environment and social meaning, and a dedication to bringing about a spatial order where Black people could, as she wrote in a letter to Fuller, participate “in the birth of their own reality.”
 Though “Skyrise for Harlem” wasn’t built, it wasn’t a failure; it was part of Jordan’s larger, lifelong literary project, and one that speaks with resonant clarity in this  summer of uprisings against police brutality and other forms of anti-Black racism. What she called her “poetics of form” contested racist metaphors that linked Black people to blight. (Moses’s plan for the Cross Bronx Expressway, for example, mobilized a bundle of racist ideas in order to garner support for his project.) The world that produced the urgent need for a “Skyrise for Harlem” was the same one that foreclosed its realization. But as the geography professor and prison abolitionist Ruth Wilson Gilmore has written, “What the world will become already exists in fragments and pieces, experiments and possibilities.” The fragments of Jordan’s experiment might be sutured to our own broken-open moment.
title: 当琼·乔丹和巴克敏斯特·富勒试图重新设计哈莱姆区时
title_en: When June Jordan and Buckminster Fuller Tried to Redesign Harlem

note:
十多年前，这个价值3亿美元的按钮的故事在一本名为“网页表单设计：填补空白”(Web Form Design：Fill in the Blanks)的书中首次被报道时，引起了巨大的轰动。我最近偶然发现了它，立刻就被迷住了。
这是一个很好的例子，说明了设计是如何影响一项业务的，在这种情况下，影响的金额高达3亿美元。它也提醒人们，默认是如何重要的，如何塑造用户行为，甚至影响品牌和平台之间的力量平衡。
只有两个按钮：登录和注册。会出什么差错？与世隔绝时，这些按钮是足够无辜的。但第一个危险信号是，他们在用户点击“结账”后立即出现。
这家据传是百思买(Best Buy)的公司聘请了一名可用性研究员。在测试中，用户很高兴地将东西添加到购物车中，进入结账…。然后就僵住了。第一次使用的用户很恼火，他们必须注册才能完成购买：“我不是来这里谈恋爱的。我只是想买点东西。“。重复使用的用户也很恼火。他们中的大多数人都不记得自己的登录名和密码了。一些人使用了忘记密码流程，但很难记住它是在哪封电子邮件下。
他们测试的改进很简单。他们把“注册”按钮换成了“继续作为客人”，并附上了一些关于接下来会发生什么的令人欣慰的内容：
结果令人瞠目结舌：订购用户的数量增加了45%，第一年就多赚了3亿美元。更好的是，90%的订购用户选择在结账完成过程中开户。
这不是对客人结账发明的颂歌。虽然这是这个特定情况下的解决方案，但这个故事背后更普遍的教训是：
理解用户的期望--在某人刚刚花费精力创建订单之后要求他们“注册”是不恰当的时机和糟糕的框架。提出大要求的最佳时机是当你把它与一些非常有价值的东西联系在一起时(例如，获得一项特殊功能的访问权限)，或者当它已经整合到他们想要做的行动中时(例如，填写一个地址来接收他们的购买)。你不想要心怀不满的用户，因为他们不会信任你，不会留下来。
跟踪点击数据-该公司没有跟踪任何点击。当他们最终开始记录事件时，他们发现大多数用户在看到“注册”和“登录”后都放弃了。可用性研究挽救了他们，但衡量转化率会更早敲响警钟。
以用户为中心是一项很好的业务--最吸引人的情节转折是，几乎所有的用户在看到这样做的价值时(例如，接收发货更新)，最后都很乐意注册。获取可持续业务价值的最佳途径是创造更多的用户价值。
Snapchat对着镜头打开，TikTok向你打开，Instagram将故事放在馈送的最顶端，这是有原因的。他们在声明什么是对产品重要的，但更重要的是，他们知道这会改变人们的行为。大多数用户坚持默认设置，并且默认设置使用得越多，效果就越好。
搜索已经成为互联网的默认设置。这是理解这个新的富足世界的第一个解决方案。亚马逊将搜索作为其强大的Everything商店的门户。但有没有更好的解决方案呢？
本周，在看了TikTok上的狂热评论后，我在Insta上订购了boba冰淇淋。在TikTok上看到瑞士一家田园诗般的酒店后，我也很想预订一家。所有这一切听起来很滑稽，但它回避了一个问题，即房间里是否有新的、强有力的违约。
搜索可以给你任何你内心想要的东西。BROWSE有潜力为您提供您内心想要的任何东西…。在你知道要不要它之前。搜索仍然是在任何给定时刻显示你想要的东西的最快途径，但我们现在正处于生活的早期，当产品比你自己更了解你时，我们的生活是什么样子。
在许多方面，浏览更类似于线下世界，在那里，你可以进行偶然的对话和发现，这些东西会给你带来超出预期的灵感。商人乔把一个平凡的杂货店变成了一个寻找花椰菜团子的愉快的寻宝活动。总部位于中国、市值870亿美元的拼多多(Pduoduo)也牢记这一点，它创建了一款社交电商产品，其基础是浏览产品信息源，并与他人分享以锁定储蓄。
另一方面，亚马逊迎合了那些更喜欢完全避免购物的人。您在搜索框中键入，使用一次单击结账，安排循环顺序，您就完成了。不需要浪漫。
那些真正喜欢购物的人只剩下一百万个质量各异的不同网站。这是一个巨大的聚合机会，从Instagram到Shopify，许多玩家都在关注这一机会。无论谁接管TikTok的美国业务，都将坐拥电子商务潜力的宝库。
你寻找已知的，却偶然发现了新的。搜索帮助富人变得更加富有。毕竟，大多数用户不会冒险通过前几个结果。浏览打开了精英管理的大门。当然，体验仍然可以被操纵以有利于某些玩家，但有更多的空间来真正发现新事物。
在互联网出现之前的世界里，品牌拥有一切权力，因为它们是守门人。当互联网到来时，搜索栏成了事实上的看门人。你仍然可以搜索品牌，但它们的力量是以前的自我的外壳-它们完全依赖于你记住它们的名字。在一个基于浏览的世界里，品牌力完全消失，转移到抓住需求的玩家手中。
这并不是说伟大的品牌就不能突破。如果他们获得了很高的参与度(或聚合器认为最好的任何指标)，他们仍然会占上风，但完全依靠名字将不再是一个可行的选择。所有这些都表明，质量比以往任何时候都更重要，如果你真的想保持你的能力，你应该与你的用户建立直接的关系。
一个伟大的产品就像一个组织良好的管弦乐队，从产品设计到商业模式和数据模型，所有的乐器都是相辅相成的。愚蠢的设计和没有数据分析可能会让你付出数百万美元的代价，也会让你的用户失去信任。选择默认设置可能会改变用户行为，甚至会影响力量平衡。明智地选择。：)
note_en:
The story of the $300M button made a big splash over a decade ago when it was first covered in a book called  Web Form Design: Filling in the Blanks. I recently stumbled upon it and was immediately captivated.
 It’s a great example of how designs can impact a business, in this case to the tune of $300M. It’s also a reminder of how  defaults matter, shape user behavior, and even influence the balance of power between brands and platforms.
  There were only two buttons: Login and Register. What could go wrong? In isolation, the buttons were innocent enough. But the first red flag was they showed up right after a user clicked “Checkout”.
 The company, rumored to be Best Buy, hired a usability researcher. In the tests, users happily added stuff to their cart, made their way into checkout… and then froze. The first-time users were annoyed they had to register to complete their purchase: “I’m not here to be in a relationship. I just want to buy something.” The repeat users were also annoyed. Most of them couldn’t remember their login and password. Some used the forgot password flow, but struggled to remember which email it was under.
 The improvement they tested was simple. They replaced the “Register” button with “Continue as Guest”, and included some comforting copy on what would happen next:
   The results were eye-popping: the number of ordering users went up by 45%, resulting in an  extra $300M that first year. Even better, 90% of the ordering users chose to make an account as part of the checkout completion process.
  This isn’t an ode to the invention of guest checkout. While that was the solution in this specific instance, the more universal lessons behind this story are:
 Understand user expectations — asking someone to “register” after they just spent energy creating an order is poor timing and poor framing. The best time to make a big ask is when you tie it to something very rewarding (e.g., getting access to a special feature) or when it’s already integrated into an action they want to do (e.g., filling out an address to receive their purchase). You don’t want disgruntled users because they won’t trust you and stick around.
 Track click data — this company was not tracking any clicks. When they finally started logging events, they found that most users bailed after seeing “Register” and “Login”. The usability study saved them, but measuring conversion would have sounded the alarms much earlier.
 Being user-centric is good business — the most fascinating plot twist was that nearly all the users were happy to register at the end when they saw value in doing so (e.g., receiving shipping updates). The best path to capturing sustainable business value is to create a lot more user value.
  There’s a reason why Snapchat opens to the camera, TikTok opens to the For You feed, Instagram places Stories at the very top of the feed. They are making a statement about what’s important to the product, but more importantly, they know it shifts behavior. Most users stick to defaults, and defaults get better the more they are used.
 Search has been the default for the internet. It was the first solution to make sense of this new world of abundance. Amazon uses search as the portal to its mighty everything store. But could there be a better solution?
 This week, I ordered boba ice cream on Instacart after watching a rave review on TikTok. I’ve also been tempted to book an idyllic hotel in Switzerland after seeing it on TikTok. All of this sounds comical, but it begs the question of whether there’s a new, powerful default in the room.
 Search gives you whatever your heart desires and asks for.  Browse has the potential to give you whatever your heart desires… before you know to ask for it. Search is still the fastest path to manifesting what you want at any given moment, but we’re now in the early days of what life is like when products know you better than you know yourself.
 In many ways, browsing is more akin to the offline world where you have serendipitous conversations and discoveries that inspire you beyond your expectations. Trader Joe’s turns a mundane grocery run into a delightful treasure hunt for cauliflower gnocchi. Pinduoduo, an $87B company based in China, has also taken this to heart, creating a social ecommerce product based on browsing a feed of products and sharing with others to lock in savings.
 Amazon, on the other hand, caters to people who prefer to avoid shopping altogether. You type into the search box, use one-click checkout, schedule recurring order, and you’re done. No romance required.
 Those who actually enjoy shopping are left with a million different websites of varying quality. This is a massive aggregation opportunity that a number of players are eyeing from Instagram to Shopify.  Whoever takes over TikTok’s U.S. operations will be sitting on a treasure trove of e-commerce potential.
  You search for the known, you stumble upon the new. Search helps the rich get richer. After all, most users do not venture past the first few results. Browse opens the gates to meritocracy. Of course, the experience can still be rigged to favor certain players, but there is more room for true discovery of new things.
 In the pre-internet world, brands had all the power because they  were the gatekeepers. When the internet arrived, the search bar became the de facto gatekeeper. You can still search for brands, but their power is a shell of its former self — they’re entirely dependent on you remembering their name. In a browse-based world, brand power vanishes entirely and shifts to the player that captures the demand.
 That’s not to say great brands can’t break through. If they receive high engagement (or whatever metric the aggregator deems to be best), they will still prevail, but exclusively relying on the name will no longer be a viable option. All this to say that quality is more important than ever, and if you’re really serious about maintaining your power, you should build a direct relationship with your users.
 A great product is like a well-organized orchestra where all the instruments complement each other, from product design to the business model and data model. Silly designs and no data analysis can cost you millions and the trust of your users. Selecting your default can shift user behavior and even influence the balance of power. Choose wisely. :)
title: 3亿美元的按钮和其他课程

note:
从最小的粒子到分子，模拟有多种类型，代表了更大的缩放因子。每种类型都假定前一阶段(类型)的物理特性，该物理特性被重用以实现可伸缩性。例如，驻波物理在相1时空中证明了在相2粒子中的效率。
每个模拟都有自己的面板，其中包含用于控制模拟的各种UI选项。更改UI控件后，通过选择“Run X”按钮开始模拟，其中X是模拟类型。在每种模拟类型中运行的UI选项和建议的步骤在其自己的ReadMe中进行了说明，其中每个阶段文件夹都可以在根目录附加zip文件中找到。
note_en:
The simulation has multiple types, representing a greater zoom factor, from the smallest particles to molecules. Each type assumes the physics of a previous phase (type) which is re-used for scalability. For example, the physics of a standing wave is proven in Phase 1 Spacetime for efficiency in Phase 2 Particles.
 Each simulation has its own panel with various UI options to control the simulation. After changing UI controls, the simulation is started by selecting the “Run X” button, where X is the simulation type. The UI options and suggested steps to run in each simulation type is addressed in its own ReadMe, where each phase folder can be found in the root directory add-on zip file.
